text: The surgeon begins the sigmoid colectomy by removing omental adhesions from the deep pelvis, revealing the omentum attached to the colovaginal fistula. The video shows the clean deeper pelvis and the fistula itself, followed by the drainage of a small abscess in the cervix and the visible defect in the colon.

The surgeon marks the distal aspect of the proximal bowel for safe anastomosis, then detaches the colon from the left pelvic side wall to initiate medial to lateral dissection. The retroperitoneal plane is entered, and the superior hemorrhoidal artery is isolated and sealed using a vessel sealer. The colon is not very redundant, and the surgeon follows the white line to connect with the previously dissected medial plane.

The surgeon identifies the ureter and joins the previously medially dissected plane. The mid-sigmoid colon, adhered to the left side wall, is detached. The lateral dissection, including splenic flexure mobilization, is completed. The surgeon prepares to divide the mesentery distally. The procedure involves natural orifice extraction using four eight-millimeter ports, with the specimen extracted transvaginally due to the cervix's presence and the smaller vaginal opening.

The surgeon marks the margins for transection with cautery after checking ICG perfusion. The proximal and distal divisions of the bowel are made. An EEA sizer is inserted into the vagina, and a posterior vaginotomy is performed to facilitate specimen extraction. The cervix is visible during the procedure.

The surgeon dilates the vaginal opening to check for additional pus before closing it using a Tua PDS suture. The closure is performed to prevent loss of pneumoperitoneum. Subsequently, the surgeon performs a sewn bowel anastomosis using a two-layer technique, with a two-PDS suture for the posterior layer and a three OV lock for the inner layer, starting posteriorly and running around the sides.

This is finishing off the anterior inner row. This patient did exceedingly well with the four eight millimeter trocars and a tap block at the end of the surgery. There was no need for any narcotics during the hospital stay after leaving the recovery room and discharged on postoperative day two without any narcotics. I have to say, I found that the transvaginal extraction for this was significantly more easier than a transrectal extraction. I think a lot of it just has to do with how forgiving it is with the tension pulling the specimen through without the need to morselate it, which can take a lot of time. You do need to then close the vaginal opening, and there are a lot of patients, at least when I talk to them about it, candidates for it preoperatively. I have not found that they are willing to do it, and I won't do it without having discussed it with them. But I did think that it worked really, really nicely.

The surgeon runs a Tua PDS suture as the outer anterior row, tying it to the posterior outer row loop. A leak test is performed, showing no leak and good distension of the proximal bowel without tension. The surgeon fills the pelvis with omentum, suturing it in place to keep the anastomosis separate from the vaginal closure. The omentum is sutured into the defect in the cervix, using residual sutures to occlude the opening completely.
